items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Vague doubts haunt me once again...
Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation and preserves the mock-heroic phrasing of «терзают смутные сомнения» plus the clipped parallelism of the two objects. It also avoids the misleading possessive “has,” matching the reference’s key nuance that these are the items associated with each person (and now missing).

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation keeps the core comic mechanism: Bunsha’s anachronistic, repetitive, overly polite Soviet-style self-introduction while impersonating the Tsar. It slightly expands the repetition (“very pleased to meet you” appears more than in the reference), which can dilute the punchy, mechanical feel of the original staccato pattern, but the joke remains clear and performable.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; preserves the sudden authoritarian, power-hungry register that is funny coming from Bunsha.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; keeps the elevated/archaic melancholy and the insulting mismatch (“old woman”) that drives the humor in a modern Soviet domestic setting.

items.translation: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; preserves the subject-role of “demons” and the panicked, superstitious framing of a mundane technical situation.

items.translation: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; retains the mock-biblical proclamation and the religious ‘miracle’ reading that is especially pointed in the Soviet atheist context.

items.translation: The floor is open to everyone!
items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; captures the emcee/announcer vibe and the comic release of decorum.

items.translation: Now, this was a good call!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; keeps the breezy, self-satisfied criminal cheerfulness and the ‘lucky find’ vibe that fits the Soviet deficit-goods subtext.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; crucially avoids calling the eggplant spread “caviar,” preserving the intended Soviet-food-shortage satire and preventing confusion for non-Russian viewers.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; preserves the comic inflation-by-enumeration and the self-important “domestic” qualifier, plus the trailing “Three” that signals the speaker’s muddled exaggeration.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; keeps the calm, rhythmic, fatalistic refrain that turns a frightening reality (psychiatric commitment) into shared absurdist comedy.

summary.interpretation: 0.09 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.02 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
